summary: P037 is a first-year international management student at Bath living in Quads halls with 9 flatmates. She presents as conscientious and health-focused, cooking elaborate meals from scratch twice weekly with careful attention to nutritional balance (protein, carbs, vegetables). Her food relationship is characterised by a tension between her health aspirations and the practical realities of student life - she spends up to 1.5 hours making dinner while stressing about lost study time and delayed sleep. She's embraced online grocery delivery as a time-saving solution, ordering weekly either individually or in small groups with flatmates, primarily through supermarket websites rather than delivery apps. Her approach to food delivery is highly strategic and price-driven - she will switch platforms for any amount of savings and actively hunts for vouchers and discounts. Despite her health-conscious cooking, she treats restaurant delivery as a weekend indulgence, triggered mainly by offers, special occasions, or when she doesn't feel like cooking. Notably, she has unique insight into the delivery ecosystem, having worked as an Uber Eats driver with her mother during COVID - an experience that made her acutely aware of the economics and time investment involved.
personality_notes: Eager to please and somewhat uncertain in conversation, asking for validation ('Is that good?'). Methodical and health-conscious but also pragmatic about student constraints. Price-sensitive to an extreme degree - will switch apps for £1-2 savings. Organised with meal planning but stressed about time management.
food_relationship: Health-conscious home cook who uses delivery strategically for convenience (groceries) and treats (restaurant food), with strong price sensitivity driving all decisions.

Health-conscious identity conflicts with student budget constraints, creating internal tension
Group ordering behaviour may be driven more by cost-splitting than social bonding
Weekend delivery treats may be psychological reward for weekday cooking discipline

_citations.cited_text: Do you want to know about, like, my hobbies as well? Is that good?

So what we've done more often recently is doing food orders together. It kind of saves some time from going into town and doing a food shop that way. So we'll... if it's from, like, Sainsbury's or Tesco's, we'll all have, like, one computer, and then one person will do the big order, and then we'll separate the cost.
But other than that, I use, like, online food orders, like, probably weekly. I think it's so much more convenient!

spk_1: So, I generally cook everything from scratch, or at least I try to be a little bit more health-conscious. So, I'll usually try and get, you know, every single component: like I get my protein, my carbs, and my vegetables. And if it's not vegetables in that meal, then I'll have fruit in place of that to eat after.
I make it all myself rather than having freezer food or pre-packaged food. I think it's just like... it makes me feel better about the food choices that I'm making.

So, for example, last night I think I spent, like, an hour and... like almost an hour and a half making dinner. And that's, like, stressful because I'm like, I could be getting work done right, right now! And I was like, this is pushing back my sleep as well. So that's definitely the most stressful meal of the week.
spk_1: I feel like... any amount. Any amount of money... yeah.
spk_1: I actually was a writer once. Yeah, I did it... like, this is actually so funny. I did it during COVID with my mom.
But we, like, did that for a whole two days. Yeah, just me and my mom. Like, it was kind of a fun experience. Yeah!
